intake.remote_work_facts: Worked entirely from home in NJ; never visited NY office

task.issue: Application of NY convenience-of-employer rule to remote NJ-based work for NY employer.

analysis: Loaded skills/tax-research-state-income/references/states/NY.md (status: stub at v1.0.0-beta) and skills/tax-research-state-income/references/states/NJ.md (status: stub). New York's convenience-of-employer rule (TSB-M-06(5)I, codified in 20 NYCRR §132.18(a)) treats wages of a non-resident as NY-source if the employer's office is in NY UNLESS the work outside NY is for the employer's necessity (not the employee's convenience). Most remote-work-from-home is treated by NY as 'convenience' rather than 'necessity'. Result: NY taxes substantially all of $190K. NJ resident credit for taxes paid to NY available under N.J. Rev. Stat. §54A:4-1. The credit limits double taxation but does not eliminate NY's claim.
conclusion: NY can tax substantially all of the $190K under the convenience-of-employer rule (TSB-M-06(5)I; 20 NYCRR §132.18(a)). NJ resident credit available for taxes paid to NY (N.J. Rev. Stat. §54A:4-1) limits double taxation but does not eliminate NY's claim. State confidence capped at LOW because both per-state files are `status: stub`; recommend live retrieval from NY DTF and NJ DOR. Practitioner should walk through any 'employer's necessity' arguments (e.g., bona-fide home-office test under TSB-M-06(5)I) but the bar is high.
band_rationale: Per-state files are `status: stub`; conclusion caps at LOW until promoted. NY convenience rule is well-established but nuanced; live retrieval recommended.
